concepts.title: Pascal's Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Pascal's Law

It states that "pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the wall of the containing vessel".

A well known application of Pascal's law is the hydraulic lift used to support or lift heavy objects. It is schematically illustrated in figure.

 

A piston with small cross section area ${{A}_{1}}$ exerts a force ${{F}_{1}}$ on the surface of a liquid such as oil.

The applied pressure is $\text{P}=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{1}}}{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}$ transmitted through the connecting pipe to a larger piston area ${{A}_{2}}$. The applied pressure is the same in both cylinders so

$\text{P}=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{1}}}{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{2}}}{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}\text{ or }{{\text{F}}_{2}}=\frac{{{\text{A}}_{2}}}{{{\text{A}}_{1}}}\cdot {{\text{F}}_{1}}$

Now, since ${{A}_{2}}>{{A}_{1}}$, therefore, ${{F}_{2}}>{{F}_{1}}$. Thus, hydraulic lift is a force multiplying device with a multiplication factor equal to the ratio of the areas of the two positions. Dentist's chairs, car lifts and jacks, many elevators and hydraulic brakes all use this principle.

concepts.title: Barometer
concepts.conceptBody:
Barometer

It is a device used to measure atmospheric pressure.

In principle, any liquid can be used to fill the barometer, but mercury is the substance of choice because its great density makes possible an instrument of reasonable size.

${{\text{P}}_{1}}={{\text{P}}_{2}}$

Here, and ${{\text{P}}_{1}}=$ atmospheric pressure $\left( {{\text{P}}_{0}} \right)$

${{\text{P}}_{2}}=0+\rho \text{gh}=\rho \text{gh}$

$\rho =$ density of mercury

${{\text{P}}_{0}}=\rho \text{gh}$

Thus, the mercury barometer reads the atmospheric pressure $\left( {{\text{P}}_{0}} \right)$ directly from the height of the mercury column.

For example if the height of mercury in a barometer in $760\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ mm}$, then atmospheric pressure will be,

${{\text{P}}_{0}}=\rho g\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}$

$~=\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 13.6 & {{10}^{3}} \\ \end{array} \right)\left( 9.8 \right)\left( 0.760 \right)$

$~=1.01\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{10}^{5}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Manometer
concepts.conceptBody:
Manometer

 

It is a device used to measure the pressure of a gas inside a container.

The $U$ - shaped tube often contains mercury.

${{\text{P}}_{1}}={{\text{P}}_{2}}$

Here, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{P}}_{1}}=$ pressure of the gas in the container $\left( \text{P} \right)$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{P}}_{2}}=$ atmospheric pressure $\left( {{\text{P}}_{0}} \right)+\rho \text{gh}$

$\text{P}={{\text{P}}_{0}}+\text{h}\rho \text{g}$

This can also be written as

$\text{P}-{{\text{P}}_{0}}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ gauge }\!\!~\!\!\text{ pressure }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{h}\rho \text{g}$

Here, $\rho $ is the density of the liquid used in U-tube.

Thus by measuring $\text{h}$ we can find absolute (or gauge) pressure in the vessel.

concepts.title: Free Body Diagram of Liquid
concepts.conceptBody:
Free body diagram of a liquid 

The free body diagram of the liquid (showing the vertical forces only) is shown in Fig. (b). For the equilibrium of liquid.

Net downward force $=$ net upward force

${{\text{P}}_{0}}\text{ A}+\text{W} =\left( {{\text{P}}_{0}}+\rho \text{ gh } \right)\text{A}$

$\text{W}=\rho gh\text{ A}$

 

Example

A liquid of density $\rho $ is filled in a beaker of cross section S to a height H and then a cylinder of mass m and cross section S is made to float in it as shown in Fig. If the atmospheric pressure is ${{p}_{0}}$, find the pressure (a) at the top face A of the cylinder (b) at the bottom face C of the cylinder and (c) at the base B of the beaker. Can ever these three pressures be equal? 

Solution: 

(a) Above the cross section A there is external pressure due to the atmosphere only.

So ${{\text{p}}_{\text{A}}}=\text{ Atmospheric }\text{ pressure }={{\text{p}}_{0}}$

(b) At the point $\text{C}$ the pressure will be due to atmosphere and also due to the weight of the cylinder, i.e.,

${{\text{P}}_{\text{C}}}={{\text{P}}_{0}}+\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{S}}$

Note : If $\text{h}$ is the height of cylinder inside the liquid, by concept of hydrostatic pressure ${{\text{p}}_{\text{C}}}$ must be ${{\text{p}}_{0}}+\text{h}\rho \text{g}$ so that

${{\text{p}}_{0}}+\left( \text{mg}/\text{s} \right)={{\text{p}}_{0}}+\text{h}\rho g\text{, }\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\text{ mg}=\text{hs}\rho \text{g}=\text{V'}\rho \text{g}\left[ \text{ as }\text{ V'}=\text{hS} \right]$

i.e., weight of cylinder is equal to the thrust, which is the principle of floatation and verifies the correctness of our result.

(c) At the point B of beaker, pressure will be due to atmosphere, weight of cylinder and weight of liquid in the beaker; so

${{\text{p}}_{\text{B}}}={{\text{p}}_{0}}+\frac{\text{mg}+\text{pgHS}}{\text{S}}={{\text{p}}_{0}}+\text{pgH}+\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{S}}$

Note : The pressure due to the weight of cylinder at its own base $\text{C}\left( \text{mg}/\text{s} \right)$ is more than at the base of beaker $\text{B}\left( \text{mg}/\text{S} \right)$ as $\text{s}<\text{S}$.

If the system is in fall (as in a satellite), $\text{g}\to 0$,

${{\text{p}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{p}}_{\text{C}}}={{\text{p}}_{\text{B}}}={{\text{p}}_{0}}\left[ \text{as }\text{ weight }=0 \right]$

examples.title: The height of a mercury barometer is $75 \mathrm{~cm}$ at sea level and $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ at the top of a hill. Ratio of density of mercury to that of air is $10^4$. The height of the hill is
A barometer kept in a stationary elevator reads $76 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the elevator starts accelerating up the reading will be
The value of $g$ at a place decreases by $2 \%$. The barometric height of mercury

A barometer tube reads $76 \mathrm{~cm}$ of mercury. If the tube is gradually inclined at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical, keeping the open end immersed in the mercury reservoir, the length of the mercury column will be

A vertical U-tube of uniform inner cross section contains mercury in both sides of its arms. $\mathrm{A}$ glycerin (density = $1.3 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$) column of length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ is introduced into one of its arms. Oil of density $0.8 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ is poured into the other arm until the upper surfaces of the oil and glycerin are in the same horizontal level. Find the length of the oil column, Density of mercury $=13.6 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$

A U-tube in which the cross-sectional area of the limb on the left is one quarter, the limb on the right contains mercury (density $13.6 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ ). The level of mercury in the narrow limb is at a distance of $36 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the upper end of the tube. What will be the rise in the level of mercury in the right limb if the left limb is filled to the top with water
